genes.name: Repair Cron Delivery
genes.trigger: cron announce delivery failed
genes.strategy: Check device scopes, add missing scopes, restart gateway
genes.origin: PCEC #6 — 2026-02-21 scope-upgrade fix

genes.name: Sync README Version
genes.trigger: SKILL.md version changed
genes.strategy: Update README.md and README_CN.md version table
genes.origin: Repeated mistake — 2026-02-21

genes.name: Quote YAML Description
genes.trigger: SKILL.md frontmatter contains colons or special chars
genes.strategy: Wrap description in double quotes
genes.origin: GitHub YAML parse error — 2026-02-21

genes.name: Prune Unbounded Sections
genes.trigger: append-only section in a reference doc exceeds ~30 lines
genes.strategy: Extract historical entries to archive file, keep summary + pointer in original

genes.name: Subagent Announce Timeout
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Subagent completion direct announce failed.*gateway timeout'
genes.strategy: 1) Check gateway health. 2) Transient → log and monitor. 3) Repeated 3+ in 1h → restart gateway. 4) Chronic (same runId retrying across hours) → stuck session; the run completed but announce keeps retrying indefinitely. Cannot fix from agent side — this is a gateway retry loop. Log the pattern and move on. 5) Critical subagents: use sessions_list to check result manually.
genes.origin: PCEC #9 — 2026-02-21, updated PCEC #11, refined PCEC #13

genes.name: NPM Publish Version Conflict
genes.trigger: CI release fails with E403 'cannot publish over previously published versions'
genes.strategy: 1) npm view <pkg> versions --json. 2) Reset via changesets. 3) Monorepo: sync all packages. 4) Verify: pnpm publish --dry-run.

genes.name: Edit Tool Exact-Match Failure
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'edit failed: Could not find the exact text' or 'edit failed: Found N occurrences' or 'edit failed: Missing required parameter: newText'
genes.strategy: The edit tool requires exact text matching including whitespace. Failures happen for three reasons: (1) text not found — file was modified between read and edit, or whitespace/newline mismatch; (2) multiple occurrences — text is not unique enough; (3) deletion attempt — newText is empty or whitespace-only, which the tool rejects as 'Missing required parameter'. For case (3), use the merge-replace pattern: match the target block PLUS adjacent content, then replace with only the adjacent content (effectively deleting the target while keeping surroundings). For JSON files (<50 lines): use write (full overwrite) instead. For markdown/large files: read the file first, use longer unique context in oldText, or use write if the file is small enough. Never guess at whitespace — always read before editing.
genes.origin: PCEC #11 — 2026-02-22 patrol-state.json, widened PCEC #16 for MEMORY.md, hardened PCEC #19 for deletion case

genes.name: Subagent Telegram Target Resolution
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Unknown target.*for Telegram' or 'Explicit message target required' or 'Delivering to Telegram requires target <chatId>'
genes.strategy: Subagents spawned from cron/non-chat contexts lack implicit Telegram chatId. Never use target='main' — Telegram needs numeric chatId. For cron tasks needing to message: use sessions_spawn (auto-announces to requester). For direct message: omit target to let the framework route, or use sessions_send to relay through parent session.
genes.origin: PCEC #12 — 2026-02-22 repeated subagent announce failures

genes.name: Message Tool Missing Required Params
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: messageId required' or 'message failed: target required' or 'message failed: Action <action> requires a target' (where <action> can be send, read, thread-reply, or any message tool action) or 'message failed: send requires text or media'
genes.strategy: message actions react/edit/delete require messageId. Thread actions (thread-reply, thread-create) require target (the thread/channel id). Read action (read) requires target (the channel/thread id to read from). send requires at least one of: message (text content) or media/filePath/buffer (attachment). Always check the action's required params before calling: send needs target + (message or media), read needs target, react needs messageId+emoji, edit needs messageId+message, delete needs messageId, thread-reply needs target+message (target is the thread id). The general pattern 'Action X requires a target' applies to ANY action that needs a target channel/thread — not just send and thread-reply. 'send requires text or media' means message.send was called with neither text nor attachment — always provide at least one. When target is unavailable (e.g. in cron/subagent context with no implicit chatId), use sessions_send to relay through the main session instead of message.send directly. When messageId is unavailable (e.g. in cron context), skip the action gracefully. For thread-reply specifically: the target must be the threadId, not a channel or user — check thread-list first if the thread id is unknown.
genes.origin: PCEC #13 — 2026-02-22 react called without messageId, hardened PCEC #22 for 'Action send requires a target', hardened PCEC #39 for thread-reply variant, hardened PCEC #48 for 'send requires text or media', hardened PCEC #53 for general Action-requires-target pattern (read action)

genes.name: LLM Provider Cascade Failure
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'All models failed' or multiple lanes timing out simultaneously with 'LLM request timed out' + 'HTTP 502 upstream_error', or multiple embedded runs ending with 'stream_read_error' within minutes
genes.strategy: 1) Detect: multiple lanes (main, cron, session) all failing with timeout/502/stream_read_error within minutes. 2) Root cause: all configured models share the same upstream provider, so a single outage kills everything. Three failure modes: (a) timeout — no response at all, (b) 502 upstream_error — provider returns error, (c) stream_read_error — provider starts responding but SSE stream breaks mid-response. All three indicate provider instability. 3) Immediate: nothing to do — system auto-recovers when provider comes back. Queued cron jobs will batch-fire (expect duplicates). 4) stream_read_error specifically: the run's partial response is lost, agent sees isError=true. The run will be retried on next trigger. Often precedes or follows LLM timeouts as the provider degrades. 5) Prevention: ensure failover models use different upstream providers (e.g., deeprouter + astralor should point to different Anthropic endpoints, or mix in a non-Anthropic model like MiniMax for critical cron). 6) The PCEC cron itself is affected — expect batched triggers after recovery.
genes.origin: PCEC #14 — 2026-02-22 total outage, hardened PCEC #24 for stream_read_error variant (3 events on 2026-02-24)

genes.name: Gateway Restart Blocked by Plugin Dependency
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Gateway restart failed health checks' preceded by 'failed to load plugin.*Cannot find module'
genes.strategy: 1) Identify the blocking plugin: grep 'failed to load plugin' in log. 2) The plugin's npm dependency is missing (e.g., @larksuiteoapi/node-sdk for feishu). 3) If plugin is not actively used: remove or disable it in gateway config to unblock restarts. 4) If plugin is needed: install the missing dep with npm/pnpm. 5) This is critical — a broken plugin blocks ALL gateway restarts, preventing recovery from any issue. 6) Notify human if the agent cannot resolve (dependency install may need elevated permissions).
genes.origin: PCEC #15 — 2026-02-22 feishu plugin blocked 8 gateway restarts

genes.name: Internal Subsystem Auth Mismatch
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'No API key found for provider' from an internal subsystem (e.g., llm-slug-generator, diagnostic lane)
genes.strategy: Internal gateway subsystems (slug-generator, diagnostics) may hardcode a provider (e.g., 'anthropic') that doesn't match the configured auth profiles (which use proxies like deeprouter/astralor). This is a gateway config issue, not an agent-fixable problem. 1) Confirm: the error provider doesn't appear in auth-profiles.json. 2) Non-critical — slug generation falls back gracefully. 3) To fix: either add a direct API key for the provider, or wait for a gateway update that routes internal subsystems through configured proxies. 4) Log and suppress — don't treat as actionable error in PCEC.
genes.origin: PCEC #16 — 2026-02-22 slug-generator fails 'anthropic' auth in 3 consecutive cycles

genes.name: Gateway Restart Health Check Race Condition
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Gateway restart failed health checks' WITHOUT preceding 'failed to load plugin'
genes.strategy: 1) Check: did the gateway actually start? grep for 'listening on ws://' after the error timestamp. 2) If 'listening on' found: this is a benign race condition — the restart orchestrator's health check ran before the new instance was fully ready. No action needed; system self-healed. 3) Verify: check subsequent cron runs succeeded (confirms gateway is operational). 4) If 'listening on' NOT found: real failure — investigate for OOM, port conflict, config parse error, or other startup blockers. 5) Key differentiator from gene_gateway_restart_blocked_by_plugin: plugins loaded OK, no 'Cannot find module' errors. 6) Do not restart gateway again — the transient case means it already recovered.
genes.origin: PCEC #20 — 2026-02-23 restart at 00:28 UTC, health check failed but gateway started normally 1s earlier

genes.name: Cron Tool API vs Internal Storage Format Mismatch
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'cron failed: invalid cron.update params' or 'cron failed: invalid cron.add params' with 'unexpected property' errors
genes.strategy: The cron tool API schema differs from the internal jobs.json storage format. Common mistakes: (1) Using 'message' instead of 'text' in payload — the tool API requires payload.text, not payload.message. (2) Using 'id' instead of 'jobId' for cron.update — the tool requires jobId at the root. (3) Nesting model/agentId/timeoutSeconds/thinking directly in payload — these may be top-level tool params, not payload sub-fields. (4) payload.kind may need a specific value or be omitted entirely. Never read jobs.json and pass its format directly to the cron tool — always check the tool's own parameter schema first. If unsure of the correct format, use 'openclaw cron --help' via exec to discover the CLI API, or try cron action:list to see the tool's response format.
genes.origin: PCEC #21 — 2026-02-23 main session failed 4x trying to update dev-mgr patrol job with internal storage format

genes.name: Web Search Rate Limit (Brave 429)
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'web_search failed: Brave Search API error (429)' or 'RATE_LIMITED'
genes.strategy: Brave Search Free plan has strict limits: 1 request/second rate limit and 2000 queries/month quota. When hitting 429: (1) Do NOT immediately retry — wait at least 2 seconds. (2) Fall back to web_fetch with a search engine URL (e.g., web_fetch('https://www.google.com/search?q=...')) as a backup. (3) For concurrent tool calls that include web_search, stagger them or reduce to a single search. (4) Check meta.quota_current in the error response to monitor monthly usage. (5) If quota is near 2000, prefer web_fetch over web_search for the rest of the month. (6) This error is transient and self-resolving (rate limit clears in seconds), but quota exhaustion persists until month reset.
genes.origin: PCEC #23 — 2026-02-23 search rate-limited at 03:31 UTC during gateway docs browsing

genes.name: Session Transcript Auto-Repair (Synthetic Error Result)
genes.trigger: session history contains '[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; inserted synthetic error result for transcript repair'
genes.strategy: A tool call was recorded but no result came back. The gateway auto-repairs by inserting a synthetic error result (isError: true). Common causes: (1) gateway restart during tool execution, (2) LLM cascade timeout causing session abort while tool is in-flight, (3) process crash or OOM during tool call. Single occurrences: benign, auto-repair worked, no action needed. Clustered (3+ within minutes): correlates with a system event — check for concurrent gateway restart errors or LLM timeouts at the same timestamp. The affected tool call's result is lost; the agent sees isError:true and should retry if the operation is safe and idempotent. No prevention available — this is a race condition between session persistence and tool execution lifecycle.
genes.origin: PCEC #21 — 2026-02-24, observed 11 occurrences across 6 sessions (Feb 3–24), clusters correlate with LLM cascade failure (Feb 23) and gateway restarts

genes.name: Stale Skill Paths After Installation Migration
genes.trigger: gateway log shows '[tools] read failed: ENOENT' for a skill SKILL.md path referencing a defunct installation directory (e.g., /root/.nvm/versions/node/...), or system prompt <available_skills> locations don't match actual OpenClaw install path
genes.strategy: After migrating OpenClaw installation (NVM→system, or NVM auto-version-bump), skill paths in the system prompt may reference the old installation directory. Diagnosis: (1) Verify actual install: 'which openclaw' → should resolve to /usr/bin/openclaw with files at /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/. (2) Check if referenced paths exist — if NVM was cleaned up, old paths are dead. (3) Primary fix: 'openclaw gateway restart' — skill paths are resolved at gateway startup from the binary's __dirname. (4) If stale paths persist after restart: an OpenClaw auto-update may have reinstalled under NVM (auto-updater uses npm which follows NVM if active in shell). Run 'openclaw doctor --repair' to fix systemd service paths, then restart again. (5) Nuclear: uninstall from old location ('npm uninstall -g openclaw' in NVM shell if it still exists), verify system install is correct, restart gateway. (6) Related signal: 'Invalid config' warnings about unknown channels/plugins often co-occur during the same upgrade transition — these indicate openclaw.json references features that changed names between versions. Run 'openclaw doctor' to reconcile. (7) Prevention: after any major upgrade or installation path change, always verify skill paths work by reading one skill SKILL.md.
genes.origin: PCEC #26 — 2026-02-25 ENOENT for coding-agent SKILL.md at /root/.nvm/v24.13.0/ (path doesn't exist, OpenClaw installed at /usr/lib/). NVM fully removed but system prompt still injects old paths.

genes.name: Config Schema Validation Failure After Upgrade
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Config validation failed:' followed by a Zod-style error (e.g., 'Invalid input: expected record, received array' or 'expected string, received number')
genes.strategy: OpenClaw upgrades may change the expected config schema. The gateway validates openclaw.json at startup and logs a hard ERROR if a field's type changed. Diagnosis: (1) Parse the error path (e.g., 'channels.discord.guilds') and type mismatch ('expected record, received array'). (2) Read the current config with 'cat ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json | python3 -c ...' to inspect the offending field. (3) If already fixed: the field was corrected manually or by openclaw doctor between the error and now — just capture the gene. (4) If still broken: transform the field to match the expected type. Common migrations: array→record (wrap array items as record values keyed by id), string→object (wrap in {value: old_string}), flat→nested. (5) After fixing: 'openclaw gateway restart' to re-validate. (6) Impact assessment: the gateway may still start with fallback defaults for the invalid field, but behavior will be degraded (e.g., guild-specific settings ignored). Check if the affected channel/feature is working by testing a send/receive. (7) Prevention: after any 'openclaw upgrade', run 'openclaw doctor' before restarting — it can detect and auto-fix common schema migrations. (8) Distinct from gene_stale_skill_paths_after_migration: paths are about file resolution; this gene is about config value types/structure.
genes.origin: PCEC #29 — 2026-02-25 'channels.discord.guilds: expected record, received array' after OpenClaw 2026.2.23 upgrade. Gateway started with fallback, config later corrected.

genes.name: Discord Event Listener Timeout
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'EventQueue.*Listener.*timed out after \d+ms for event (MESSAGE_CREATE|INTERACTION_CREATE)'
genes.strategy: The gateway's internal event queue gives each listener a timeout to process incoming Discord events. Two variants: (A) DiscordMessageListener — 30s timeout for MESSAGE_CREATE (incoming messages). (B) InteractionEventListener — 120s timeout for INTERACTION_CREATE (slash commands, button clicks, select menus). Timeout means the session or LLM response took too long to process the event. Diagnosis: (1) Check for concurrent LLM timeout/502 errors at the same timestamp — if present, the provider was slow and this is a downstream symptom (cross-ref gene_llm_provider_cascade_failure). (2) Check if the session was handling complex multi-tool work (agent setup, heavy file operations) — session contention causes the listener to queue behind in-flight work. (3) For INTERACTION_CREATE specifically: Discord requires interaction responses within 3s (initial ack) — the 120s gateway timeout is for the full processing. If the gateway's initial ack fails, the user sees 'This interaction failed'. (4) Single occurrences: benign, the event may still be processed after timeout (gateway retries for messages; interactions may be lost). (5) Clustered (3+ within 30min): indicates sustained load or provider degradation — check session_status for model response times. (6) Impact: MESSAGE_CREATE — message may be dropped or delayed. INTERACTION_CREATE — slash command response lost, user sees failure. (7) Mitigation: ensure heavy background work (cron, subagent spawns) uses isolated sessions rather than the main Discord-bound session. Consider lighter models for high-traffic Discord channels. (8) Not agent-fixable: timeouts are hardcoded in the gateway event queue.
genes.origin: PCEC #30 — 2026-02-25 two MESSAGE_CREATE timeouts, widened PCEC #72 for INTERACTION_CREATE variant (2026-03-04 120s timeout)

genes.name: Discord Components v2 Invalid Block Type
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: components.blocks[N].type must be a supported component block' or 'Unknown Message' or 'Unknown Emoji' or 'Unknown Channel' or 'guildId required' from Discord message tool calls
genes.strategy: The Discord Components v2 API only accepts specific block types. When constructing a components payload, valid block types in the blocks array are: 'text' (text content), 'section' (text + accessory), 'buttons' (button row), 'divider' (separator), 'file' (attachment), 'gallery' (media items), 'select' (dropdown menu). Common mistakes: (1) Using Slack-style block names ('header', 'image', 'context', 'rich_text') — Discord doesn't support these. (2) Using 'paragraph', 'markdown', 'embed' — not valid v2 block types. (3) For text with formatting, use type='text' with markdown in the text field. (4) For images/media, use type='gallery' with items[].url. (5) For headers, use type='text' with bold markdown (**Header**) or use the container.accentColor for visual grouping. (6) 'Unknown Message' means the messageId referenced a deleted or non-existent message — verify message exists before edit/delete/react. (7) 'Unknown Emoji' means the emoji string isn't valid for Discord — use standard Unicode emoji names (e.g., '👍' not ':thumbsup:'), or for custom emoji use the {name: 'name', id: 'id'} format from emoji-list. (8) 'guildId required' means a guild-scoped action (permissions, role-info, channel-create) was called without guildId — always include guildId for guild-specific operations. Prevention: before using components, double-check block types against the valid list above. When in doubt, use plain text messages without components. (9) 'Unknown Channel' means the target channel ID doesn't exist in Discord — the channel was deleted, the bot was removed from the guild, or the ID is stale/wrong. Common causes: cron jobs or subagents referencing channels that were deleted between configuration and execution, or channel IDs copied incorrectly. Fix: verify the channel exists with channel-info before sending, update stale channel references in cron payloads and TOOLS.md. For cron jobs targeting specific channels, periodically validate that target IDs still resolve.
genes.origin: PCEC #31 — 2026-02-25 6x component block errors + 2x Unknown Message + 2x Unknown Emoji + 1x guildId required during multi-agent setup and Discord interaction, hardened PCEC #58 for 'Unknown Channel' (2026-03-01 12:01 UTC)

genes.name: Message Tool Media Path Restriction
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: Local media path is not under an allowed directory' OR 'discord tool reply failed: LocalMediaAccessError: Local media path is not under an allowed directory'
genes.strategy: The message tool and Discord reply layer restrict local file paths to allowed directories (typically ~/.openclaw/workspace/ and standard download paths). Files in /tmp, /data/images/, /root, or other arbitrary locations are rejected. Two error surfaces: (1) message tool layer — logged as 'message failed:', (2) Discord reply layer — logged as 'discord tool reply failed: LocalMediaAccessError:'. Both have the same root cause and fix. Commonly affected paths: /tmp (test/temp files), /data/images/ (AI-generated images stored by image generation tools — 8x errors on 2026-02-28 alone). Workarounds by file type: (A) Images (most common): upload to image hosting first (e.g., Chevereto via chevereto-upload skill), then send the hosted URL via message — this completely bypasses path restrictions and is the preferred workflow for sharing AI-generated images. (B) Binary files: use the buffer parameter with base64 content (e.g., read file → base64 encode → pass as buffer) — bypasses path restrictions. (C) Text/script files: copy to workspace first (cp /data/file ~/.openclaw/workspace/file), or paste inline in message text. (D) Prevention: when generating images for sharing, either write directly to workspace, or plan for the upload-then-URL workflow from the start. (E) Never symlink or hardlink restricted paths into workspace — the restriction is a security measure against exfiltration of system files.
genes.origin: PCEC #32 — 2026-02-25 /tmp test images, hardened PCEC #52 for /data/images/ path + Discord reply layer trigger (8x on 2026-02-28)

genes.name: Process Tool Write-After-End Stream Error
genes.trigger: gateway log shows '[tools] process failed: write after end'
genes.strategy: The process tool's write/send-keys/paste action tried to write to a process session whose stdin stream has already been closed (the process exited or was killed). This is a Node.js stream race condition — the process terminates between the agent's decision to write and the actual write syscall. Each event is dual-logged (subsystem + exec module), so 2 log lines = 1 actual failure. Diagnosis: (1) Check process list around the error timestamp — the target session likely exited just before the write. (2) Common triggers: background process completes faster than expected, process killed by timeout/OOM while agent has queued input, or agent sends follow-up input after process already printed its final output and exited. (3) Impact: the write silently fails, the intended input is lost. If the process already completed successfully, this is benign (write was unnecessary). If the agent needed interactive input to continue, the session is effectively dead. Mitigation: (a) Before writing to a long-running process, use process(action='poll', timeout=100) to check if it's still alive. (b) For fire-and-forget processes (coding agents, build commands), don't send follow-up writes — use the wake-event pattern instead. (c) If write fails, check process logs to see if it already completed successfully. (d) Not agent-fixable at the root level — the gateway's process tool could add a pre-write liveness check, but currently doesn't.
genes.origin: PCEC #38 — 2026-02-26 2x write-after-end at 04:49+04:51 UTC during active session with background processes

genes.name: Web Search Parameter Validation (Brave 422)
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'web_search failed: Brave Search API error (422)' with 'VALIDATION' code, typically for invalid search_lang or country values
genes.strategy: Brave Search API validates parameter enum values strictly and returns 422 on mismatch. Common pitfall: using ISO 639-1 short codes (e.g., 'zh', 'pt') when Brave requires extended variants. Correct mappings: Chinese → 'zh-hans' (simplified) or 'zh-hant' (traditional), NOT 'zh'. Portuguese → 'pt-br' or 'pt-pt', NOT 'pt'. English GB → 'en-gb', NOT 'en-GB'. Japanese → 'jp', NOT 'ja'. Full valid list: ar, eu, bn, bg, ca, zh-hans, zh-hant, hr, cs, da, nl, en, en-gb, et, fi, fr, gl, de, el, gu, he, hi, hu, is, it, jp, kn, ko, lv, lt, ms, ml, mr, nb, pl, pt-br, pt-pt, pa, ro, ru, sr, sk, sl, es, sv, ta, te, th, tr, uk, vi. When calling web_search with search_lang for Chinese content, always use 'zh-hans'. If unsure about a language code, omit search_lang entirely — Brave defaults sensibly. Distinct from gene_web_search_rate_limit (429) which is about quota/rate, not parameter validity.
genes.origin: PCEC #36 — 2026-02-26 search_lang 'zh' rejected at 01:46 UTC, escalated L1→L2 PCEC #70 2026-03-04 (TOOLS.md warning strengthened after 6 recurrences)

genes.name: Discord Bot Missing Channel Permissions
genes.trigger: gateway log shows '[tools] message failed: Missing Permissions'
genes.strategy: The Discord bot tried to send a message (or react, edit, etc.) in a channel where it lacks the required permission (SEND_MESSAGES, ADD_REACTIONS, etc.). This typically happens when: (1) A new channel/guild is created and the bot role wasn't granted channel-level permissions. (2) Channel permission overwrites explicitly deny the bot role. (3) The bot is in a category where permissions are inherited but the channel has restrictive overwrites. Diagnosis: (a) Identify which channel triggered the error — check session context for the channel id. (b) In Discord server settings, verify the bot role has SEND_MESSAGES + VIEW_CHANNEL + READ_MESSAGE_HISTORY for that channel. (c) Check channel-specific permission overwrites (they take priority over role permissions). (d) For thread channels, the bot also needs SEND_MESSAGES_IN_THREADS. Fix: adjust channel permissions in Discord server settings, or move the bot to a role with Administrator permission for the guild. Impact: messages are silently dropped — the agent thinks it sent but the user never sees the response. Dual-logged (subsystem + exec module), so 2 log lines = 1 actual failure. Distinct from gene_message_missing_params (tool parameter issues) and gene_discord_components_v2_block_validation (payload format issues) — this gene is about Discord API authorization, not request validation.
genes.origin: PCEC #47 — 2026-02-27 3x Missing Permissions at 11:13 UTC on new Discord workspace channel 💡丨产品

genes.name: Discord Channel Type Action Mismatch
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: Cannot execute action on this channel type'
genes.strategy: Certain Discord channel types don't support all message actions. Channel types and their restrictions: (1) Voice/Stage channels — only voice-status works; send/react/thread-create/pin all fail. (2) Forum channels — cannot send directly; must use thread-create to create a 'post', then send within the thread. (3) Announcement/News channels — send works, but crossposting behavior differs. (4) Category — not a sendable channel; it's a container for channels. (5) DM vs Guild — some guild-specific actions (permissions, channel-edit) fail in DMs. Diagnosis: (a) Identify the target channel from session context. (b) Use channel-info to check the channel type (0=text, 2=voice, 4=category, 5=announcement, 10=announcement thread, 11=public thread, 12=private thread, 13=stage, 15=forum, 16=media). (c) Map the failing action to the channel type's supported actions. Fix: before executing a message action on an unfamiliar channel, check its type first. For voice channels, use voice-status instead of send. For forum channels, use thread-create instead of send. Impact: action silently fails, dual-logged (2 log lines = 1 failure). Distinct from gene_discord_missing_permissions (authorization) and gene_message_missing_params (parameter validation) — this gene is about action/channel-type compatibility.
genes.origin: PCEC #48 — 2026-02-28 6x 'Cannot execute action on this channel type' at 13:58+15:57 UTC on Feb 27 during Discord workspace setup

genes.name: Discord Target Name Resolution Failure
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: Unknown target "..." for Discord' where the target is a channel display name (possibly with emoji/CJK chars) instead of a snowflake ID
genes.strategy: The Discord message tool does NOT resolve channel display names to IDs. It requires a channel ID (snowflake, e.g., '1476874308503474267') or a prefixed format (user:ID, channel:ID). Common mistakes: (1) Using the channel display name directly (e.g., '📰丨汇总', '#general') — this always fails. (2) Copying the name from channel-list output without extracting the ID. (3) Cron tasks or subagents hardcoding channel names instead of IDs. Fix: (a) Use message(action='channel-list', guildId='...') to enumerate channels and find the target's ID. (b) Store resolved channel IDs in TOOLS.md, cron payload, or memory for reuse — names change, IDs don't. (c) For cron jobs targeting Discord channels, always embed the channel ID in the payload at setup time. (d) The hint in the error message shows valid formats: bare channelId, 'user:ID', or 'channel:ID'. Distinct from gene_subagent_telegram_target (Telegram chatId) and gene_message_missing_params (missing target entirely) — this gene is about providing a target that can't be resolved, not about omitting one.
genes.origin: PCEC #49 — 2026-02-28 'Unknown target 📰丨汇总' at 23:01 UTC Feb 27 during news aggregation cron

genes.name: Config Patch JSON Merge Patch Array Replacement
genes.trigger: gateway log shows '[tools] gateway failed: invalid config' from a config.patch call, OR agent discovers missing config entries (bindings, channel rules, allowlists) after a successful config.patch that was intended to add/modify a subset
genes.strategy: config.patch follows RFC 7396 JSON Merge Patch semantics. Critical implication: arrays are REPLACED wholesale, not merged/appended. When patching any array-typed config field (bindings, channels allowlist, auth-profiles list, etc.), the patch MUST include the complete array — omitting existing entries deletes them. Common failure modes: (1) Agent patches bindings with only new entries → existing bindings silently dropped. (2) Agent patches channel allowlist with subset → other channels lose access. (3) Agent assumes additive behavior (like array.push) but gets replacement. Workflow: (a) Always config.get first to read the current full value. (b) Merge the desired changes into the full array locally. (c) Send the complete merged array in the patch. (d) Verify after patch with another config.get. For object-typed fields, merge-patch works intuitively (keys are merged). The trap is specifically with arrays. If config.patch returns 'invalid config', the payload structure itself is malformed — check field names, nesting depth, and value types against the schema (cross-ref gene_config_schema_validation_after_upgrade for type mismatches). Distinct from gene_cron_tool_schema_mismatch (cron tool API format) and gene_config_schema_validation_after_upgrade (startup Zod validation) — this gene is about runtime patch semantics and silent data loss from array replacement.
genes.origin: PCEC #50 — 2026-02-28 config.patch 'invalid config' at 02:37 UTC during wenwen multi-account setup + bindings array replacement caused dev-mgr/dev-engineer/review-engineer bindings loss (from daily memory)

genes.name: Write/Edit Tool Workspace Sandbox Restriction
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'write failed: Path escapes workspace root: <path>' or 'edit failed: File not found: <path-outside-workspace>' where <path> is outside ~/.openclaw/workspace/ (commonly /tmp, /data, /var, etc.)
genes.strategy: The write and edit tools are sandboxed to the workspace root (~/.openclaw/workspace/). Any path outside this directory is rejected with 'Path escapes workspace root'. This causes cascading failures when a multi-step workflow (e.g., unpack→process→repack) creates temp files outside workspace — the first write fails, then subsequent edits/reads on the never-created files also fail. Common trigger: document processing workflows (DOCX unpack, image manipulation, build artifacts) that use /tmp as scratch space. Workarounds: (1) Use exec() for file operations outside workspace — shell commands (cp, mkdir, unzip, mv) are NOT sandboxed to workspace. (2) Use workspace subdirectories for temp files: create ~/.openclaw/workspace/.tmp/ and work there, then clean up after. (3) For document processing: exec('mkdir -p /tmp/work && unzip doc.docx -d /tmp/work') for unpack, exec('cat /tmp/work/file.xml') for read, exec with sed/awk for edits — keep everything in exec, not write/edit tools. (4) After processing, use exec('cp /tmp/work/result.file ~/.openclaw/workspace/output/') to bring results back into workspace. Key distinction: exec() runs shell commands with full filesystem access; write()/edit() are tool-layer sandboxed. gene_media_path_not_allowed is about message tool's media attachment paths — different tool, different sandbox, different error message. gene_edit_exact_match_failure covers edit content matching issues — this gene is about path access, not content matching.
genes.origin: PCEC #54 — 2026-03-01 write to /tmp/skill-validate blocked (3 cascading errors: write sandbox → pandoc on missing file → edit missing file) during skill-validator DOCX processing at 16:07-16:10 UTC Feb 28

genes.name: Discord Slash Command Target Format Invalid
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'message failed: Unknown target "slash:.*" for Discord'
genes.strategy: The 'slash:' prefix is an internal gateway convention for Discord slash command interaction contexts — it encodes the interaction author's user ID. It is NOT a valid target format for the message tool. When an embedded/diagnostic/cron agent receives slash command context metadata and tries to send a message, it may naively use 'slash:userId' as the target. Valid Discord targets are: bare channelId (snowflake), 'user:ID', or 'channel:ID'. Diagnosis: (1) Identify the originating session — check for 'embedded run' or 'diagnostic' in surrounding log lines. (2) The userId after 'slash:' is the slash command invoker's Discord user ID. Fix: (a) In embedded/diagnostic agents: let the framework handle response routing via direct reply (no explicit target needed). (b) If explicit messaging is required: use 'user:ID' format (replacing 'slash:' with 'user:'). (c) If the goal is to reply in the channel where the slash command was invoked: extract the channelId from the interaction metadata, not the user ID. (3) Impact: the message is silently dropped — the slash command invoker gets no response. Often followed by 'Unknown Message' errors as the agent retries with a message reference that was never created. Dual-logged (2 log lines = 1 failure). Distinct from gene_discord_target_name_resolution (channel name → ID) and gene_subagent_telegram_target (Telegram chatId) — this gene is about the 'slash:' prefix specifically.
genes.origin: PCEC #56 — 2026-03-01 diagnostic embedded agent used slash:740564786584223845 as target at 06:13 UTC

genes.name: Auto-Compaction Context Window Overflow
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Auto-compaction failed' with 'prompt is too long: N tokens > M maximum' followed by 'Restarting session'
genes.strategy: The session's accumulated context exceeded the model's maximum token limit (e.g., 200k for Claude) before auto-compaction could fire, or the compaction request itself exceeded the limit. Gateway auto-recovers by restarting the session (clearing all history) and retrying the latest message. Causes: (1) Rapid accumulation of large tool outputs (full file reads, verbose exec, long web_fetch results) faster than the compaction interval. (2) Many back-and-forth exchanges with tool-heavy workflows in a single session. (3) Session used for extended collaborative discussion with large code blocks or analysis. Impact: all session history and conversational context is lost on restart — the agent starts fresh with only the latest message. Ongoing multi-step workflows lose their state. Mitigation (agent-side): (a) Use offset/limit when reading large files — never read entire large files in one call. (b) Prefer targeted grep/head/tail over full file dumps. (c) For long-running sessions with heavy tool use, proactively suggest /new to the user before context grows critical. (d) When doing multi-step analysis, write intermediate results to files rather than keeping everything in conversation. (e) Avoid requesting full verbose output of long-running commands. Prevention (system-side): the compaction interval and trigger threshold are gateway-controlled — agent cannot adjust. Distinct from gene_session_transcript_repair (missing tool results) and gene_llm_provider_cascade_failure (provider outage) — this gene is about context size exceeding model limits.
genes.origin: PCEC #83 — 2026-03-06 session 815711d3 overflowed 204922 > 200000 tokens at 08:20 UTC during Discord thread discussion

genes.name: Thinking Level Unsupported Model Downgrade
genes.trigger: gateway log shows 'Thinking level "xhigh" is not supported for <model>; downgrading to "high".' (or any unsupported thinking level variant)
genes.strategy: 1) Identify affected cron jobs: grep cron IDs from the warning lines (format: [cron:<id>] Thinking level...). 2) Root cause: cron jobs configured with thinking: "xhigh" but the target model only supports up to "high". The gateway auto-downgrades silently but logs a WARN per invocation. 3) Impact: functional — no failure, model runs at "high" thinking. But massive log noise (15+ warnings per 4h cycle across 3+ cron jobs = 63+/day). 4) Fix: update each cron job's thinking parameter to match the model's maximum supported level. For current models: astralor/claude-opus-4-6 → "high", gptclub-openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark → "high", gptclub-claude/claude-sonnet-4-6 → "high". Use cron tool action:update with the corrected thinking value. 5) Prevention: when creating cron jobs, verify the target model's supported thinking levels. The gateway's downgrade mechanism is a safety net, not an intended workflow. 6) Verification: after fixing, grep the next day's log for "xhigh" — count should drop to zero.
genes.origin: PCEC #87 — 2026-03-07 78 warnings across Mar 6-7 (63 on Mar 6, 15 on Mar 7 first 4h), affecting 3 models across multiple cron jobs
